content:
@martin-braun Danke, I already figured that the API key is going to be misused, but I didn't want to bother setting up the app signing so I can restrict it to my app id and I will invalidate it once this is done anyway.

My current idea is to find out where exactly the exception is getting thrown, and then maybe see if we can implement a check in the platform code, to prevent the null value being passed to the api code.

Another possible workaround might be to delay the zoom animation of the initialPosition/location as opposed to delaying the navigation itself.
So that the navigation is "instant", but we use the initialPosition without zoom, and zoom in some milliseconds afterwards (if the zoom animation really is the culprit).

> So that the navigation is "instant", but we use the initialPosition without zoom, and zoom in some milliseconds afterwards (if the zoom animation really is the culprit).

In my example app I implemented some custom zoom buttons to get them on iOS as well and even when I wait a second after load I managed to crash the app on Android by hitting zoom-in and back (to pop) at the same time. Again, it only happens sometimes. So yea, simply zooming later will simply shift the time frame in which popping can cause a crash.

I think a possible band-aid would be to block popping while we wait for things to be load after initial zoom and any zoom in general, but then again, we have no callback (yet). I hate this solution, though. Imagine you have slow internet speed and cannot pop the view, because you have to download the map of your location first ...

> This is however the part where debugging further gets more complicated, since the destroy call is inside the proprietary code, and decompiling that does not really tell anything too useful. So we might need to get some google engineers involved at this point...

Yep, proprietary code, another case to praise open-source. Even if we find a band-aid, it will cause some clunky user experience. I really hate those dead-ends.

> I am not quite sure yet, what the downside to this is, but I can imagine that this may cause a memory leak or some other unintended behaviour, since we're basically removing a part of the `dispose()` code.
> 
> But atleast now we might have a more concrete idea, which part of the code is destroying something, that is still needed by another part of the code 🤷‍♂️

I don't wonder why this works and yes, it would probably be a memory leak. Something tries to access that nulled array and the array is nulled within `mapView.dispose()`. 

Remember this link I posted? https://github.com/flutter/plugins/blob/main/packages/google_maps_flutter/google_maps_flutter/android/src/main/java/io/flutter/plugins/googlemaps/GoogleMapController.java#L154

Read the comment, it peaks my interest again. Apparently, the renderer needs time to draw the map (2 frames), before it can invalidate the `mapView`. So what if the `mapView` is already destroyed, but the renderer tries to render its final frame. After all it renders only new content when there are new things to draw, i.e. on *zooming*. Sounds familiar, sounds related to me. Sometimes the renderer wins, sometimes the destroyer wins.

I think Google needs to ensure to cancel that logic in `mapView.destroy()` before nulling the subjective array.

@lukaskurz Good morning, good approach. Interesting that only one frame (not two frames) later the issue won't appear anymore. So yea, the renderer probably stops in the same frame when the view pops, but it takes two final frames (of which the first is the current) to finish its work. So there is our narrow one-frame window that causes the exception.

I wonder now, is it somehow possible to implement your band-aid purely on the dart-side, without the necessity to use a fork of the `google_maps_flutter` package? The reason for my asking is that I maintain a package and try to circumvent this issue for now. It would be way less hassle to simply have a dart-fix.
@martin-braun Hopefully the maintainers of the `google_maps_flutter` package will agree to Lukas' fix and include it in a new release, which wouldn't make necessary the use of a forked version.

@rivella50

![tenor](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/24259245/178425560-da5ed103-0d40-4223-acfb-668743507d0a.gif)

@BoLindJensen I have already created a fork at https://github.com/lukaskurz/plugins :+1: To use it, just use a dependency override in your pubspec.yaml like this:

```yaml
dependency_overrides:
  google_maps_flutter:
    git:
      url: https://github.com/lukaskurz/plugins
      path: packages/google_maps_flutter/google_maps_flutter
      ref: main
```

I am going to eat lunch now, and after that I will file the PR with the flutter team. Let's hope that we can resolve this quickly.

@martin-braun I am not quite sure if this is possible. The issue is the order in which stuff gets marked as "destroyed" and it actually being destroyed on the platform side. Postponing the `dispose` of the platform widget would not resolve that issue IMO.
@BoLindJensen Is there a way to do this with SupportMapFragment also, I am having the same problem @lukaskurz

> Experiencing the Same Error on Flutter 3.0.3 , Reverting back to 2.10.5 Fixed the issue.

It sounds like this may be a bug in the new platform view implementation then. If that's the case, then the plugin is the wrong place to fix it.

Does forcing hybrid composition, by following the plugin's hybrid composition instructions and also changing `initSurfaceAndroidView` to `initExpensiveAndroidView` in `method_channel_google_maps_flutter.dart` fix this? We're already looking at switching maps to Hybrid Composition while the new platform view behaviors are investigated further, so it would be good to know if doing so would also address this.

Is it confirmed that this crash never happens with Flutter versions 2.x.y?
Doesn't sound that reliable if we only have a statement of one user here.

@stuartmorgan I will check whether hybrid composition fixes this issue :+1:. While changing the `CHANGELOG.md`, I saw that [2.0.9 fixes](https://github.com/flutter/plugins/pull/4250) an issue which looks a lot like this one, so maybe its related.

So i just tested it, adding the `useAndroidViewSurface = true` per documentation

```dart
void main() {
  if (defaultTargetPlatform == TargetPlatform.android) {
    AndroidGoogleMapsFlutter.useAndroidViewSurface = true;
  }

  runApp(const MyApp());
}
```

And changing to `initExpensiveAndroidView` in [`method_channel_google_maps_flutter.dart:521`](https://github.com/flutter/plugins/blob/1d2cc3aa286ce6b8fb8abded23e54b0dae2628ef/packages/google_maps_flutter/google_maps_flutter_platform_interface/lib/src/method_channel/method_channel_google_maps_flutter.dart#L521)

```dart
onCreatePlatformView: (PlatformViewCreationParams params) {
            final ExpensiveAndroidViewController controller = PlatformViewsService.initExpensiveAndroidView(
              id: params.id,
              viewType: 'plugins.flutter.io/google_maps',
              layoutDirection: widgetConfiguration.textDirection,
              creationParams: creationParams,
              creationParamsCodec: const StandardMessageCodec(),
              onFocus: () => params.onFocusChanged(true),
            );
```

The error still occured, and now (in my demo app) all 4 pages trigger a crash, although it's a different exception now.

While it's a different exception, the behaviour is the same. Quick switching between pages ~ creating and disposing the widget causes an app crash.
Same here with hybrid composition activated in my app.

\

There's been some discussion in https://github.com/flutter/plugins/pull/6093; capturing the relevant part here:

> It looks at this point like there are probably two issues which result in very similar stack traces (as both are attempting to use the map after it's been destroyed):
>
> * An ordering bug with some async calls in the Android platform view implementation, which we're investigating; if that's the case, this would explain why there's a spike of issues in 3.0.
> * An internal maps threading issue in the ([non-new](https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/android-sdk/renderer)) renderer.

We're currently investigating the former.

> > * An ordering bug with some async calls in the Android platform view implementation, which we're investigating; if that's the case, this would explain why there's a spike of issues in 3.0.

Is anyone still seeing frequent crashes in a current version of master? We expect that https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/108496 fixed the problem that caused much more frequent crashes in Flutter 3.0 (as compared to 2.x).

> Do you know how can we use the new renderer GMM6?

The instructions for using the new renderer (not GMM6, which is the previous renderer) are in https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/android-sdk/renderer. Because they are global state, you should be able to add that opt-in directly in your application, without any changes needed to the plugin.

I don't know if the current beta has the fix cherry picked yet, which is why my comment above refers to `master`.

> The issue is not fixed.

[I never said otherwise](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/105965#issuecomment-1203995331); the question is whether on `master` it's happening frequently (as in 3.0) or rarely (as in 2.x).

> I think a lot of people wish a plug and play experience without tampering too much in the native code.

Changing the runner isn't "tampering", since the runner is intended to be modified by developers. But if you believe there's an audience for a plugin that enables the new renderer, you are certainly welcome to publish one.

I have tested latest version (2.1.12) and the error continues
Returning to P3, since b/242810798 was resolved by the client switching to the new renderer.

The following steps will **fix** the problem (update to new renderer in kotlin project):

1. Add this line to your app's  build.gradle: 
`implementation 'com.google.android.gms:play-services-maps:18.0.2'`
2. Import these lines to MainActivity.kt: 
```
import android.util.Log;
import com.google.android.gms.maps.MapsInitializer
import com.google.android.gms.maps.MapsInitializer.Renderer
import com.google.android.gms.maps.OnMapsSdkInitializedCallback
```
2. Add `MapsInitializer.initialize(applicationContext, Renderer.LATEST, this)` in your MainActivity onCreate method.
3. Implement  OnMapsSdkInitializedCallback interface for verify the new renderer.


**example (MainActivity.kt):**
```
package com.example.application

import android.os.Bundle
import io.flutter.embedding.android.FlutterActivity
import io.flutter.embedding.engine.FlutterEngine
import io.flutter.plugin.common.MethodChannel
import io.flutter.plugins.GeneratedPluginRegistrant
import io.flutter.view.FlutterMain
import android.os.Build;
import android.view.WindowManager
import android.util.Log;
import com.google.android.gms.maps.MapsInitializer
import com.google.android.gms.maps.MapsInitializer.Renderer
import com.google.android.gms.maps.OnMapsSdkInitializedCallback


class MainActivity: FlutterActivity(), OnMapsSdkInitializedCallback{
    override 
    fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?){
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        MapsInitializer.initialize(applicationContext, Renderer.LATEST, this)
    }

    override fun onMapsSdkInitialized(renderer: MapsInitializer.Renderer) {
      when (renderer) {
        Renderer.LATEST -> Log.d("NewRendererLog", "The latest version of the renderer is used.")
        Renderer.LEGACY -> Log.d("NewRendererLog","The legacy version of the renderer is used.")
      }
    }
}

```
4. Run & Check the logs, you should see these lines:
```
D/MapsInitializer: loadedRenderer: LATEST
D/NewRendererLog: The latest version of the renderer is used.
```

@benyameenashraf Because you can circumvent the issue by switching to the new renderer, however this is a hassle. In my opinion, this issue should remain <s>on P1 or at least P2</s> until a renderer switch is exposed in Flutter to make it easy to switch to the new renderer without further touching the native parts of the app.

> In my opinion, this issue should remain on P1 or at least P2

Please see https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Issue-hygiene#priorities. P1 and P2 are not opinion-based labels in our issue tracker, and this does not meet the criteria for either.

I haven't try [this](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/105965#issuecomment-1224473127) snippets, but setting the `minMaxZoomPreference` works for me:

```
GoogleMap(
              onLongPress: (argument) {
                // do something when maps long pressed
              },
              
              initialCameraPosition: CameraPosition(
                target: LatLng(defaultLat, defaultLong),
                zoom: 16, // make sure this set at 16 max
              ),
              onMapCreated: (GoogleMapController controller) {
                controller.complete(controller);
              }),
              },
              minMaxZoomPreference: const MinMaxZoomPreference(0, 16)) // set 0-16 level
```
